summary: This is an advice column where 'Aunt Pythia' (Cathy O'Neil) answers reader questions spanning relationships, sexuality, mathematics education, and academic ethics. The questions range from navigating expectations in sexual relationships shaped by pornography, to whether math should be taught as a contemplative journey rather than efficiency-focused, to the ethics of faculty dating graduate students. O'Neil brings her characteristic directness to each topic, emphasizing consent, communication, and questioning assumptions while weaving in personal anecdotes and mathematical philosophy.
key_points: The evolution of pornography has shifted from 'rape-dominated scenes' to 'weird but consensual extreme positions,' which O'Neil sees as progress despite ongoing concerns
Respect in relationships should be measured by how someone raises questions and handles disagreement, not by what they desire
Mathematics education should embrace meandering discovery rather than obsessing over finding shortest paths to answers - 'every person engaged in learning mathematics is themselves a mathematician'
Faculty-student dating creates uncomfortable dynamics where grad students feel like a 'captive dating pool,' but formal rules may not solve the underlying issues
Communication and explicit consent are the solutions to most relationship dilemmas - ask directly rather than assume
money_quotes: It is my earnest belief that every person engaged in learning mathematics is themselves a mathematician, rediscovering and rejoicing in the mathematics that has been understood by our culture for hundreds of years but by us as individuals for no time at all.
The man who doesn't respect you is the man who pushes this stuff on you without consultation, or who makes it your problem that you're not into it.

There's nothing logically inconsistent with thoroughly objectivizing a sexual partner during a sex act and then having a mutually respectful and thoughtful conversation about free will fifteen minutes later.
Mathematicians are also the most inefficient people in the world, because they get entirely focused on abstract rules and scenarios that almost never have a concrete application to anything.
We are so obsessed with skills-based accomplishments, we rarely spend time on why we're doing something or how someone could have come up with it in the first place.
We should all be treated as philosopher queens in this process.
key_insight: Whether in sex, mathematics, or academic ethics, the key is honest communication and respecting people's autonomy to make their own informed choices.
